coredata.dc:title: Novel enhanced detection and resolution of a nonfluorescent mixture in plasma at nanogram levels using sustainable fluorescent carbon dots and advanced chemometric models
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdullatif, Hind A.
Abdelkawy, Mohammed
Boltia, Shereen A.
Fahmy, Nesma M.
Kamal, Maha
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, advanced chemometric models were utilized to determine florescence induced by carbon dots. In an endeavor to regulate anthelmintic drug usage by facilitating the determination of veterinary formulations in animals’ biological fluids, a novel fluorometric-assisted chemometric method has been developed for detecting two nonfluorescent drugs, Ivermectin (IVR) and Clorsulon (CLR). The method relies on the linear quenching effect of the drugs on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from natural sources. Despite the significant overlap, chemometric models such as partial least squares (PLS) and artificial neural networks (ANN), assisted by genetic algorithms (GA), successfully resolved the issue and achieved high-precision recovery of both drugs. The method demonstrates a linearity range of 50–6000 ng/mL, rendering it suitable for determining both drugs in biological animal fluids. To ensure practical application, the method was applied to veterinary formulations and spiked animal plasma, yielding satisfactory results. Finally, a comparison of the proposed method with official ones revealed no significant differences. According to principles of white analytical chemistry (WAC), the method also obeys sustainability rules. The method was therefore proven to be a novel, safe and applicable alternative approach for this formulation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ivermectin
Clorsulon
Carbon dots
Fluorimetry
Sustainable chemistry
Genetic algorithm-partial least squares (GA-PLS)
Genetic algorithm-artificial neural networks (GA-ANN)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calibration and validation sets construction for multivariate methods
Optimization of parameters that affect quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical Structures of IVR and CLR.
The typical HRTEM images of the CDs.
Excitation at 242 nm vs emmission spectra of CDs, IVR, and CLR.
A co-plot showing prominent overlap between UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CLR (a) and IVR (b) and fluorescence excitation spectrum of the CQDs (c) and emmision spectrum (d).
Excitation (i) and emmsision (ii) spectra of plasma alone (a), and plasma spiked with different concentrations of IVR and CLR along with CD (b,c,d).
Determination of IVR and CLR in the validation set by the proposed models.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of IVR and CLR in Spiked Animal (Cow) Plasma by the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Novel enhanced detection and resolution of a nonfluorescent mixture in plasma at nanogram levels using sustainable fluorescent carbon dots and advanced chemometric models

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Dept., Faculty of Pharmacy, Ahram Canadian University, Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Dept., Faculty of Pharmacy, Ahram Canadian University, Cairo, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Cairo University, Giza, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, advanced chemometric models were utilized to determine florescence induced by carbon dots. In an endeavor to regulate anthelmintic drug usage by facilitating the determination of veterinary formulations in animals’ biological fluids, a novel fluorometric-assisted chemometric method has been developed for detecting two nonfluorescent drugs, Ivermectin (IVR) and Clorsulon (CLR). The method relies on the linear quenching effect of the drugs on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from natural sources. Despite the significant overlap, chemometric models such as partial least squares (PLS) and artificial neural networks (ANN), assisted by genetic algorithms (GA), successfully resolved the issue and achieved high-precision recovery of both drugs. The method demonstrates a linearity range of 50–6000 ng/mL, rendering it suitable for determining both drugs in biological animal fluids. To ensure practical application, the method was applied to veterinary formulations and spiked animal plasma, yielding satisfactory results. Finally, a comparison of the proposed method with official ones revealed no significant differences. According to principles of white analytical chemistry (WAC), the method also obeys sustainability rules. The method was therefore proven to be a novel, safe and applicable alternative approach for this formulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of antimicrobials for both humans and animals is one of the 20th century's most important historical turning points. But unfortunately, they are routinely used to prevent infections and as growth promoters [1]. As a result of excessive use and reliability, a very worrying global issue aroused, named anthelmintic resistance (AR) [2,3]. Sadly, there is no clear regulation controlling anthelmintic contamination, due to the lack of available information and methods of analysis of anthelmintic residues [4]. Thereupon, there has been an ongoing call and professional encouragement to develop efficient and cost-effective techniques, for the determination and detection of veterinary anthelmintics [5].

Ivermectin (IVR), chemically represented as (1R, 4S, 5′S, 6R, 6′R, 8R, 10E, 12S, 13S,14E, 16E,20R,21R,24S)-6′-[(2S)-butan-2-yl]-21,24-dihydroxy-12-[(2R,4S,5S,6S)-5-[(2S,4S,5S,6S)-5-hydroxy-4-methoxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-4-methoxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-5′,11,13,22-tetramethylspiro[3,7,19-trioxatetracyclo[15.6.1.14,8.020,24]pentacosa-10,14,16,22-tetraene-6,2′-oxane]-2-one, stands as one of the most widely employed anthelmintics. This macrolide belongs to the avermectin group and gained extensive usage in veterinary and human medicine approximately three decades ago. Various methods described in the literature for determining IVR include colorimetric approaches [6] chromatographic techniques [7–9], and electrochemical methods [10,11].

Clorsulon (CLR), chemically known as 4-amino-6-(1,2,2-trichloroethenyl)-benzene-1,3-disulfonamide, is classified within the benzene-sulphonamide family and is primarily employed for the treatment of adult and mature liver fluke infections. The determination of CLR in literature encompasses various methods including spectrophotometric techniques [12], chromatographic approaches [13–15] and electrochemical methods [16,17].

The veterinary formulation Paramectin Plus® consisting of IVR and CLR is an extensively used anthelmintic drug. This is due to its extraordinary effects resulting from the synergistic effect of both drugs, as they combine both nematicide and flukicide effects [18–20]. The reliance on this combination makes it crucial to have methods of its analysis controlling this usage, therefore, monitoring of those drugs in animals is mandatory [21]. The structures of both drugs are shown in Fig. 1 .

Various methods described in the literature have attempted to analyze this combination, including spectrophotometric methods [12], which, however, failed to detect low concentrations of the drugs, and HPLC methods [9,14,22],which necessitate toxic reagents and involve cumbersome and time-consuming procedures.

Spectro-fluorimetry is a very sensitive analytical technique for identifying and determining fluorescent substances at the ng or lower levels. This contrasts with liquid chromatographic methods, which necessitate costly equipment, a significant volume of hazardous solvents, skilled labor, and an extended analysis run time, spectrofluorimetric methods offer an environmentally friendly, straightforward, and economical analytical approach [23].

This study relies on carbon dots (CD) due to their unlimited potential applications in medicine as they have robust and controllable fluorescence emission properties. Carbon dots are nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm. These materials have garnered significant interest owing to their remarkable luminescence, high quantum yield (QY), low toxicity, and availability from multiple affordable sources [24,25]. Moreover, CDs can be doped with heteroatoms like N or S to achieve their optimum optical and electrical properties [26,27].

Usually, an overlapping problem arises during the simultaneous measurement of two similar analyte spectrums, according to the analytical chemistry theory [28]. This made it crucial to increase data order by multivariate analysis methods, without the need of prior separation. Chemometrics are powerful tools for analyzing spectral data, and their ability to resolve highly overlapped spectra yields faster and very accurate results [29]. Many algorithms are used in chemometric methodologies, ranging from multivariate regressions (like Partial Least Square-PLS) to machine learning approaches (Artificial Neuron Network-ANN) [30]. Moreover, Variable selection is a popular preprocessing method used to enhance the prediction capability of chemometric algorithms. One example is Genetic algorithms (GA), which apply survival of the fittest strategy among wavelengths to decide which are most significant for building the chemometric models, thus avoiding noise and unwanted interference [31].

One of the main goals this study was keen to achieve were the sustainable goals. Green and sustainable fluorescence spectroscopy (FS) approach is achieved by using more ecofriendly solvents, recycling solutions, and using less harmful apparatus as possible. The notion of white analytical chemistry (WAC) has recently emerged as a means of evaluating the cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness, and efficiency of analytical techniques in sample analysis [32]. This study used RGB model to evaluate the whole method’s sustainability [33].

The novelty of this work lies in the pioneering application of advanced chemometric models to analyze fluorescence induced by carbon dots (CDs) for the simultaneous detection of CLR and IVR, a binary mixture of nonfluorescent drugs. This is the first time that chemometric techniques have been employed in conjunction with the fluorescence generated by CDs, allowing for the precise differentiation and quantification of drugs with significant spectral overlap.

The proposed method is groundbreaking due to its sustainable and cost-efficient nature. The CDs were synthesized using a one-step, microwave-assisted process, utilizing eco-friendly materials like lemon and onion juice. This not only avoids the need for complex and expensive instrumentation or derivatization but also ensures the method is aligned with green chemistry principles.

Moreover, the integration of chemometric models to resolve the overlapping spectra of CLR and IVR marks a significant advancement in analytical methodologies. Traditional methods struggle with such spectral overlap, but by utilizing chemometrics, this study provides an innovative solution, enabling accurate detection even at low concentration levels. The method was successfully applied to veterinary formulations and spiked animal plasma, demonstrating its practical utility.

By merging eco-friendly CD synthesis with cutting-edge chemometric analysis, this research introduces a novel approach that opens up new possibilities for detecting nonfluorescent drugs, especially in complex mixtures, in a sustainable and efficient manner.
Ivermectin, with a purity of 100.11 ± 1.019, and CLR with a purity of 99.90 ± 0.125 according to USP [34] were sourced from Pharmswede Veterinary Company in Cairo, Egypt.

Acetonitrile, NaOH, methanol and ammonium hydroxide were obtained from Sigma Aldrich in Steinheim, Germany. Lemon and onion were purchased from a local market in Giza, Egypt. Double-distilled Water was obtained by Aquatron automatic water still (Stuart, UK).
Parametcin Super® injectable solution (Batch No. 230648) where each 1 mL contains 10 mg of IVR and 100 mg of CLR, was provided by Pharma Swede-Egypt Veterinary Company in Cairo, Egypt.

The fluorescence spectrofluorometer utilized in this study was a Cary Eclipse equipped with an Agilent Xenon flash lamp and a 10-mm matched pair of stoppered quartz cells. Data acquisition and analysis were conducted using Cary Eclipse software developed by Agilent Technologies in the USA. Centrifugation was carried out using a model 2-16P Centrifuge manufactured in Germany. A domestic microwave oven, Jac NGM-2525 Microwave with a power rating of 1450 W, was employed for synthesis purposes. To perform dialysis, a FREY Scientific® dialysis 21.6 × 34 mm tube of 1KDa was used.

For high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) measurements, a JEM-2100 electron microscope was utilized to determine particle size. UV–VIS spectrophotometric measurements were performed using an ACER compatible computer connected to a double-beam UV–VIS spectrophotometer model J-760 manufactured by Jasco in Japan, employing 1.00 cm quartz cells. Spectra were recorded within the range of 200–600 nm at 0.1 nm intervals, utilizing SpectraManager® software.

The multivariate methods employed in the analysis were executed using MATLAB R2023b (version 23.2.0.2485118). Genetic algorithm (GA), partial least squares (PLS), and artificial neural network (ANN) analyses were conducted using MATLAB Toolboxes.

For the preparation of natural-source carbon dots (CDs), lemon juice (20 mL), onion juice (2 mL), and ammonium hydroxide (10 mL) were mixed with 80 mL of distilled water in a beaker. The mixture was then subjected to microwave irradiation at 1400 W for 6 min, resulting in the formation of a brown-black agglomerated solid. After microwave treatment, 100 mL of distilled water was added to the solid, and centrifugation was carried out for 45 min at 4000 rpm. The solution obtained after centrifugation was dialyzed using a dialysis tube with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1 kDa against distilled water for 24 h. Finally, evaporation of the dialyzed solution led to the formation of highly fluorescent and stable CDs, which were dispersed in distilled water for subsequent use [35].

To prepare the CDs solution, carbon dots (CDs) were dispersed in distilled water at a concentration of 500 mg/mL. For the preparation of standard solutions, 10 mg of each drug was individually added to a 100 mL volumetric flask and filled up with methanol to obtain stock solutions. Subsequently, each drug's working solution was prepared by adding varying concentrations to 10 mL volumetric flasks and completing them with methanol.

To each aliquot, 1 mL of the natural CDs was added along with various IVR and CLR working solutions ranging from 50 to 6000 ng/mL, all within a 10 mL volumetric flask and filled up with methanol. The relative fluorescence intensity was then recorded at 354 450, and 510 nm respectively for the CDs, IVR, and CLR with an excitation wavelength of 242 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration and validation sets construction for multivariate methods
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration set was constructed using a five-level two-factorial design, with 25 mixtures coded from −2 to 2. These mixtures were prepared by adding the designated concentrations along with 1 mL of natural source CDs and completing them with distilled water. The emission of the mixtures was determined by the spectrofluorometer at 354 nm with an excitation wavelength of 242 nm. Subsequently, the data was processed using MATLAB. Genetic algorithm (GA) was employed to select variables and assess their impact on the effectiveness of the models. GA-based partial least squares (GA-PLS) and genetic algorithm-based artificial neural network (GA-ANN) models were then constructed.

To evaluate the prediction ability of the models, an independent validation set consisting of 8 mixtures, which included concentrations within the linearity range, was utilized. Data below 550 nm were discarded to reduce noise and facilitate accurate predictions by the model.

In a 100 mL volumetric flask, 1 mL of the Paramectin Super® formulation, containing 10 mg of IVR and 100 mg of CLR, was measured and filled up to the mark with methanol. Then, 1 mL of this preparation was transferred to another 100 mL volumetric flask along with 1 mL of natural source CDs and topped up with distilled water to achieve a concentration of 1:10 µg/mL.

A six mL sample of drug-free male cow plasma was collected. In a 10 mL volumetric flask, 1 mL of plasma and 1 mL of acetonitrile were combined and filled up to the mark with distilled water to prepare the blank sample. Separately, different concentrations of IVR and CLR within the linearity range were added, along with 1 mL of plasma, 1 mL of acetonitrile, and 1 mL of natural source CDs, and the flasks were topped up with distilled water to the mark. The mixtures were then centrifuged to separate the precipitate at 6000 rpm for 25 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected, and 1.5 mL was withdrawn for further analysis using the studied method. The corresponding regression equation was applied to calculate the percentage recoveries of IVR and CLR.

Two 10 ml volumetric flask of quinine sulfate (indicator solution) in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 as a reference standard [36], and multiple CDs concentrations (test solution) were prepared to ensure absorbances below 0.1. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission at λex 242 nm of the indicator and test solutions of at least three concentrations were recorded. Then these adjusted spectra were used to obtain the integration peaks values.

The literature search revealed no fluorometric green or economical method to determine this combination at low concentrations. This work represents the first spectrofluorimetric-assisted-chemometric approach for the determination of IVR and CLR simultaneously, which represented the novelty of this study.

To overcome the non-fluorescence problem, various carbon dots (CDs) sourced from synthetic and natural origins, including polyethylene glycol (PEG) and CDs derived from egg white (SF.1), were experimented but yielded unsatisfactory results. However, upon investigating CDs fabricated from lemon and onion juice [35], promising fluorescence was achieved (SF.2), as there was a linear quenching effect on the CDs’ fluorescence upon altering drug concentrations (SF.3).

Onion juice (especially yellow onions) was proven to significantly increase the QY of lemon juice (a rich citric acid source), due to the N- and S-doping caused by onion juice. Sulphur atom produces more passivated surface detection of CQDs, therefore enhancing the photoluminescence. On the other hand, nitrogen atom creates a new surface state (N-state) where electrons get trapped, promoting a combination of higher yield [37].

The morphology of both prepared carbon dots (C-dots) was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image in (Fig. 2 ) reveals a uniform dispersion of spherical-shaped CDs with an average diameter ranging from 4 to 7.5 nm.

The optical properties of the CDs in distilled water were investigated, demonstrating excitation-dependent emission behavior, consistent with previous reports [35]. The emission gradually shifted to higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelengths increased from 200 to 400 nm. Notably, the most prominent emission was observed upon excitation at 242 nm and 352 nm. Readings at 242 nm provided better linearity and sensitivity and enabled measurements at lower concentrations, and the spectra is depicted in (Fig. 3 ), along with the emission spectra of IVR and CLR. The CDs exhibited a bright blue color under UV light at 365 nm and appeared light yellow in daylight, which goes hand in hand with literature [35].

The UV–VIS absorption spectrum (SF.4) displayed a shoulder at 218 nm, attributed to π–π* aromatic sp2 transitions. Additionally, a broad peak at 270 nm was observed, likely related to n–π* CO bond transitions [38,39]. The CDs’ spectrum was also investigated in buffer, showing a shift in the spectrum which could be attributed to the pH change (SF.4) [40].
There are multiple theories that could be attributed to the CDs and drugs’ interaction including:

Is one of the non-irradiation energy conversions caused by absorbers in the detection system absorbing the excitation and/or emission radiation of fluorophores, applicable only if the absorbers’ absorption spectra overlap with the excitation and/or emission spectra of the fluorophore. IFE-based sensing strategy is an effective method for converting analytical absorption signals to fluorescence signals [41].

Static quenching occurs due to the ground state complex formation, while in dynamic quenching, the quencher (IVR and CLR) moves in the excited state and encounters the fluorophore. If providing kq (quenching rate constant) greater than the maximum diffusion rate constant, then the quenching mechanism is proved to be static instead of dynamic and vice versa [42].

Is a phenomenon in which energy is transferred between molecules through dipole–dipole interactions. This transfer occurs from the excited state of the donor molecule to the acceptor molecule, without radiation emission. The emission spectra of the donor must overlap with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor. Furthermore, the difference in energy between the lowest energy level and the first electronic excited state of the donor and acceptor must be compatible [43].

It was illustrated in (SF.3) that CDs’ fluorescence experienced a noticeable reduction when adding increased concentrations of each drug. So, to identify the possible mechanism for this quenching, a methodological approach was followed. At first, CDs’ excitation and emission spectra, as well as the UV–VIS spectra of both CLR and IVR (the quenchers) were investigated and found a prominent overlap between excitation spectra of CDs and UV–VIS spectra of the drugs (Fig. 4 ). This made the IFE theory legible in our case. To further explain, CDs’ fluorescence intensity in presence of linearly increasing CLR or IVR concentrations was amended for potential IFE using the following Eq. (1) [44]:
(1)
F
corr
=
F
obs
x 10
A
ex
+
A
em
/ 2
Fcorr: corrected fluorescence intensity; Fobs: observed fluorescence intensity; Aex and Aem: absorbance of the quencher at λex and λem of fluorescent probe.

Then the suppressed efficiency (%E) was calculated for both observed and corrected fluorescence using this Eq. (2):
(2)
% E =
1 -
F
cor
/
F
obs
∗ 100

(SF.5) showed the CDs-Drug (Quencher)’s observed and corrected fluorescence, indicating that the fluorescence quenching in our study is mainly attributed to the IFE. It is possible that other mechanisms mentioned above govern the remaining portion of the quenching. The CO and NH groups in both drugs allowed strong adherence to the surface of the CDs’ hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, forming hydrogen bonds, and decreasing the distance between the drugs and CDs, thus leading to a successful FRET pair [43]. Finally, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups found in the CDs may have interacted with CLR and IVR, resulting in quenching of the CDs’ fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters that affect quenching
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the ideal circumstances, a variety of factors influencing the CDs' fluorescence sensing have been examined and interesting results were obtained, indicating that several factors could be altered to determine the optimum results. All parameters are illustrated in (SF.6) and (SF.7).

The fluorescence intensity was examined over a pH range of 4–11.5 and illustrated in (SF.6a). The highest intensity was observed at pH 7.1. Intensity levels below pH 7.1 were relatively low, while that above pH 7.1 were higher but not as pronounced as at pH 7.1. This result goes parallel with literature [43], and might be a consequence of the chemical structure of CDs. Neutral pH was found the most optimum medium to work with to resist any change, as any change also caused a bathochromic or hypochromic shift.

Several solvents were tested during the experimentation, including distilled water, methanol, buffer solution, and sodium hydroxide and represented in (SF.6b). The buffer solution at pH 6.9 yielded the lowest intensity, followed by methanol. When attempting to measure intensity with NaOH, the intensity improved, but the optimum intensity was observed with distilled water. These findings are also shown in (SF.8).

The microwave power played a critical role in the fabrication of CDs [45,46]. Generally, higher microwave power led to enhanced CDs emission, a narrower energy bandgap, and increased intensity, attributed to an escalation in the percentage of burn-off [46]. Various powers were experimented with, including 900, 1200, and 1450 W, with the latter yielding the most favorable outcomes and is represented in (SF.6c).

Furthermore, it is widely acknowledged that microwave duration affects CDs synthesis, as it could impact size, morphology, and even properties of the CDs. Relatively short duration would not produce CDs. On the other side, relatively long duration may yield larger carbon dots, as the increased exposure allows the growth of CDs resulting to an increased size and allows them to come together forming clusters [47]. Several microwave durations were tried to determine the optimum time to yield acceptable CDs. As illustrated in (SF.6d), three, six, ten and twelve minutes were investigated, and six minutes gave the best CDs.
When applying this method to the laboratory mixtures, the severe overlap found in both emission spectrum made it impossible to recover each drug alone without chemometric modeling approach.

The calibration set was constructed using a five-level two-factorial design, with mixtures coded from −2 to 2 (ST.1). Several models have been tried like PCR or PLS, but both gave inconvenient recoveries due to the severe overlap, so the need of artificial intelligence intervention was required.

The Genetic Algorithm (GA) is an optimization method used to identify the most relevant subset of measured variables for a precise regression model, drawing inspiration from natural selection theory [43]. In this study there is a prominent variable overlap in datasets due to the similar spectra of both drugs, making it difficult to identify each drug’s contribution alone by human eye. If there is too many noise and irrelevant variables, much noise is introduced to the model affecting the analysis, and if some informative values are excluded, the model’s performance suffers. That is why a variable selection tool like GA was crucial for enhancing the quality of the selected data, leading to better models for further analysis(PLS and ANN).

Appropriate tuning of the GA parameters is a critical aspect to ensure fruitful GA performance. Starting with raw data matrix with dimensions 200 × 2, multiple trials were conducted to determine the optimal.

Regarding population size, a larger population size provides a better representation of variables but requires more time. Population sizes of 50, 100, 150, and 200 were tested, with a population size of 100 yielding the lowest RMSEP for both drugs (see ST.2).
The window width, indicating the number of neighboring variables grouped together, was tested at values of 1, 2, and 4, with 1 being optimal for both drugs.

The initial term percentage determines the number of variables in the initial subset, with a lower percentage making identification more challenging. An initial term percentage of 30 was chosen for the model.
Other optimal parameters included a mutation rate of 0.005 and single breeding. A high mutation rate increases the risk of including unrepresentative variables.

The optimal number of latent variables (LV),3 and 6, was determined based on previous Partial Least Squares (PLS) trials. The percentage population at the same convergence was set at 20, with a maximum of 100 generations to avoid overfitting.
Contagious cross-validation was selected, dividing the data into four subsets, with each generation's iteration number set to 1.

After optimizing the parameters (ST.2), the subset size decreased from 200 to 60 for IVR and 66 for CLR. The resulting data were saved and used as inputs for PLS and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models, as they were deemed most valuable. This reduction of noise and non-useful information by nearly 30 %, which proves that GA successfully overcame the prominent variable overlap of the datasets.

Partial Least Squares (PLS) is a factor analysis method that concurrently analyzes responses and concentrations [48]. The model development yields the ultimate latent variables (LVs) for each drug, a critical factor in preventing overfitting [29]. In this study, the optimal LVs were determined via leave-one-out cross-validation, selecting LVs of 3 and 6 for IVR and CLR, respectively (SF.9), having the least RMSECV. All data used for model construction underwent preprocessing, employing mean centering or autoscaling. As depicted in Table 1 , the GA-PLS model encountered difficulty in obtaining recovery percentages for some validation set mixtures, attributable to certain calibration data falling outside acceptable ranges, likely due to significant drug overlap. Inaccurate results were eliminated following the elimination rule [49].

Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) are computational models inspired by the human brain's neural network, capable of learning patterns from data and approximating complex functions [50]. ANNs operate through a feed-forward neuron-based structure comprising input, hidden, and output layers. Through trial and error, optimal parameters including the number of hidden layers, training functions, and transfer function pairs were determined to achieve the best prediction outcomes. To avoid overfitting, 4 hidden layers were adequate to build the neuron.

The transfer function depends on the nature of the studied data and computes a layer's output from its net input. Among various trials using transfer function pairs such as; tansig-purelin, purelin-purelin, and logsig-purelin, purelin-purelin was identified as optimal, indicating a linear correlation between absorbance and concentration, as demonstrated in (SF.10) and (SF.11). The network was trained using multiple training functions, with Bayesian Regularization yielding the lowest root mean squared error of prediction (RMSEP).
To prevent overfitting, eight validation sets was incorporated into the training process, and ANNs stop when the calibration set RMSEP decreased and that of validation set increased.

Regarding the number of hidden layers, various values were tested, and the RMSEP values were compared. It was determined that 3 hidden layer neurons known to be adequate in solving related complications were optimal for IVR, while 5 hidden layer neurons were optimal for CLR(SF.12). Table 1 illustrates that GA-ANN successfully recovered all validation set recoveries, showcasing the artificial intelligence capabilities of these models.

After evaluating both GA-PLS and GA-ANN results, GA-ANN did not give any inaccurate results to be omitted as GA-PLS did. This is mainly attributed to the training process and several factors ANN possesses to output more accurate data. Although GA-PLS required somehow less steps than GA-ANN, the later model gave more acceptable results.

The statistical parameters of the chemometric models are outlined in Table 2 . No instances of overfitting were observed in the proposed calibration models, evidenced by the closely matching values of root mean squared error of prediction (RMSEP). A comparison of the obtained results with the official methods outlined in the USP [34]. Table 3 revealed no significant differences, affirming the predictive capability of the developed models.
The models proposed were successfully applied for the determination of both drugs in their marketed veterinary formulation with accepted recoveries and SDs as shown in (ST.3).

The study was further applied in spiked cow plasma to determine the ability of the proposed method to determine IVR and CLR in biological fluids. The maximum peak plasma of subcutaneous injection of IVR is 50 ng/mL [51] and CLR is 5250 ng/ml [52]. Fig. 5 shows the excitation and emission spectra of plasma and the cited drugs. The plasma preparations were done and analyzed as mentioned before. Satisfactory percentage recoveries ranging from 96 %-118 % were obtained and shown in Table 4 . This indicates the success of the method and reliability.

The ratio of photons released to photons received is known as the fluorescence quantum yield. It sheds light on the mechanism of a reaction by showing the ratio of desired process’s rate to all other competing unimportant processes [53]. The Qy could be easily determined using a Qy reference, with this reference having absorbance and emission wavelength in the same range as the drug. The comparative method was employed to measure the quantum yield (Qy), utilizing quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 as a reference standard [36]. To mitigate inner filter effects, concentrations of both natural source CDs and quinine sulfate were prepared to ensure absorbances below 0.1. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission and absorption of at least three concentrations of both preparations were computed to ensure experimental error elimination. The emission of each concentration was plotted against its corresponding absorbance for both CDs and quinine sulfate. The slope of the calibration curve for each preparation was then utilized in the following Eq. (3):
(3)
Q s
=
Q r
Slope
o f
C D s
Slope
o f
r e f
η s
η r
2
where “Q” denotes the quantum yield, “S” denotes CDs, “R” denotes quinine sulfate, and η represents the refractive index, which in aqueous solutions equals 1. The quantum yield of quinine sulfate (Qyr) is known to be 0.54. Through this calculation, a high CDs quantum yield of 0.56 was determined.

White Analytical Chemistry (WAC) can be conceptualized as a green approaches’ expansion, with an advantage of evaluating whether the proposed method is “fit-for-purpose” on the economical aspect as well as sustainability aspect [54–57]. This study used the RGB 12 model [58–60]. This white analytical approach demonstrates states that combining red, green, and blue light beams, standing for analytical, ecological, and practical features, creates the appearance of whiteness. Convenient scores for each of the 12 parameters are according to the considerations of the authors of WAC [32].

In the red region (analytical performance), the proposed method achieved a high score of 98.7, reflecting its excellent analytical results. It received full marks (100.0) for both scope of application (R1) and limits of detection and quantification (LOD & LOQ) (R2), indicating its wide applicability and sensitivity. Precision (R3) and accuracy (R4) were also highly rated, with scores of 98.0 and 96.6, respectively. These scores highlight the method's reliability and its ability to detect the drugs at very low concentrations, reaching the maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) of both CLR and IVR.

In the green region (green chemistry), the method scored 73.3, a satisfying but lower result compared to the analytical performance. The lowest score (30.0) was assigned to toxicity of reagents (G1) due to the use of methanol and acetonitrile, which are hazardous solvents. However, the amount of reagents (G2) was scored at 90.0, as only 200 mL of methanol was used, which is considered efficient. Energy consumption (G3) earned a score of 80.0, as the microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs was energy-efficient. Direct impact (G4) scored 93.3, reflecting the minimal waste produced, user safety (with precautions for methanol handling), and no involvement of genetically modified organisms (GMOs).

In the blue region (practical side), the method scored 93.3 overall. It is highly cost-effective (B1), with a score of 98.0, as the CDs are made using inexpensive lemon and onion juice. Time-efficiency (B2) received a score of 90.0 due to the quick fluorometric and chemometric processes, although the dialysis step for CDs fabrication required a bit more time. Requirements (B3) scored 90.0, as the method doesn’t require advanced instrumentation and can be easily applied to various matrices, despite chemometrics software posing a slight challenge. Operational simplicity (B4) was rated at 95.3, as the method is generally simple but with some difficulty related to the portability of CDs fabrication.
The method’s overall sustainability score was 88.4, demonstrating its adequacy and promising potential for sustainable applications, as shown in Fig. 6 .

Upon comparing the proposed method with the ones mentioned in literature, the spectrophotometric method mentioned [12] failed to detect the drugs at low concentrations, as the study’s linearity range was 1–23 µg/mL and 1–14 µg/mL for CLR and IVR respectively. Also, HPLC methods had linearity range of 2.5–7.5 μg/ml for IVR and 25–75 μg/ml CLR [9], 4–140 and 5–50 μg/mL for CLR and IVR with dihydrogen phosphate and acetonitrile as a mobile phase [14], and 100–400 μg/ml and utilized hazardous chemicals like acetonitrile and methanol as a mobile phase [22]. All were unlike the proposed study which mainly depended on natural renewable sources and distilled water, detected the drug at nanogram levels, and used no extensive equipment and was very fast.

This study is a significant novel step forward in analytical chemistry as it combined carbon dots’ florescence with advanced chemometric models. The cited drugs gave no fluorescence so the use of natural source carbon dots for fluorescence detection were mandatory. Then, due to the severe overlap of both spectrums, conventional regression equations did not resolve them apart. So chemometric techniques like Partial Least Squares (PLS) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) aided by Genetic Algorithms (GA), appeared to offer a robust method for determining the concentrations of drugs in IVR and CLR for the first time. The GA-PLS model was much superior to the conventional PLS model as the GA helped filter so much unwanted data, focusing only on the valuable ones. This couldn’t have been done by hand as the severe overlap obstructed seeing this by human. But also, GA-PLS gave some inaccuracies. The best solution model was GA-ANN, as it worked on non-noisy data, the most fit for resolving were trained, which gave the best results in all the previous conventional and non-conventional trials.

One of the remarkable aspects of this method is its ability to detect drugs at very low concentrations that reaches the Cmax of both drugs in plasma, which is crucial for detecting the drug in different matrices and will be further inspected in future studies. The successful application of this method in both veterinary combinations and spiked cow plasma suggests its versatility and potential for practical use in various settings.
Not applicable.

Hind A. Abdullatif: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mohammed Abdelkawy: Supervision. Shereen A. Boltia: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Nesma M. Fahmy: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Maha Kamal: Writing – review & editing.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
No funding was received.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
